mappings.rationaleAI: The largest oceanic region (A.3) with oceanic physiography (A.3.b), containing the Mariana Trench as a major deep-sea trench (D.1), part of the Circum-Pacific tectonic system (F.2.a), and seamount chains relevant to oceanic ridges (G).

The third-largest oceanic division (A.3), with narrow continental shelves (C) and youngest geological origin relevant to ocean basin formation (D). Separated from Tethys ~20 Mya, connecting to Tethyan system history.

Lakes of tectonic, volcanic, and glacial origin occupy fault-bounded structural depressions (B.2.c), including endorheic basins with no outflow. Rift-zone and mountainous lake clusters connect to continental platform depressions.

Covers solid terrestrial surface formation through plate tectonics and cratons (B), terrain types including mountains, deserts, plains, and plateaus relevant to hypsography and physiography (A.1, A.2), and continental crust composition.
The smallest oceanic division (A.3) with geology and oceanographic connections to Atlantic and Pacific relevant to ocean basin components (D).

Elevated landforms formed through tectonic forces, volcanism, or erosion (F.1), distributed within the worldwide system of mountain ranges and fracture zones (F.2). Types include fold, block-fault, and volcanic mountains.
The continuous saltwater body covering ~70.8% of Earth (A.3), including continental shelf and slope features (C), ocean basin components and depth zones (D), and oceanic ridges (G).

The second-largest oceanic division (A.3), featuring the Mid-Atlantic Ridge as a major deep-ocean floor feature (D.1.b) and the paradigmatic example of the global oceanic ridge system at a spreading-centre zone (G.1.a).

An intercontinental sea between Europe, Asia, and Africa (A.3), nearly landlocked with unique geological history including the Messinian salinity crisis, functioning as an elongated sea formed by crustal processes (B.2.c).

The unifying theory explaining ocean basin origins through seafloor spreading (D.2), the worldwide system of mountain ranges at convergent boundaries (F.2), and oceanic ridges as manifestations of divergent lithospheric plate boundaries (G.2.b).

Volcanic mountains form at tectonic plate boundaries and above mantle plumes (F.1), with volcanic island arcs at convergent boundaries constituting a major component of the worldwide fracture zone system (F.2.d).

Large terrestrial geographic units with geological basis in continental crust (A.1), physiographic descriptions of continents (A.2), and the stable platform regions including shield areas and their structure (B).
The polar region north of the Arctic Circle, relevant to the physiography of northern continental territories (A.2) and permafrost tundra surface features.
Earth's radially layered interior, including the distinction between oceanic and continental crust (D.1.a) and the continental shield areas with their age, structure, and constituent rocks (B.1).

The natural science of Earth's rock and mineral composition, internal structure, and processes broadly underpins all aspects of physical surface features, from hypsography (A) to stable platforms (B), ocean basins (D), mountain ranges (F), and oceanic ridges (G).

The longest continental mountain range formed by subduction-driven uplift (F.1), part of the Circum-Pacific system (F.2.a) and connecting to the Tethyan orogenic belt through continental collision zones (F.2.b). Includes seismic activity and volcanism.
A major folded mountain range formed through Indian-Eurasian plate collision (F.1), part of the Tethyan orogenic system (F.2.b), containing the highest terrestrial peaks.

The world's largest inland body of water occupying a structural depression (B.2.c), an endorheic basin straddling Europe and Asia with ancient connection to Paratethys, exemplifying water bodies occupying fault-bounded depressions.

Landmasses formed through plate tectonics, volcanism, and coral reef accretion relevant to coastlines and land distribution (A.1), coral reef islands and atolls (E), and volcanic island arc systems (F.2.d).
Five interconnected freshwater lakes of glacial geological origin, constituting water bodies occupying structural depressions (B.2.c) on the continental platform.
Saltwater bodies covering most of Earth's surface (A.3), with continental shelf features (C) and deep-ocean floor structures including trenches and abyssal plains (D).
The largest mountain system in North America formed by Laramide orogeny (F.1), representing a subsidiary mountain range within the global system (F.2.c).
Rivers shape the vertical relief and coastline features of Earth's surface through erosion, sedimentation, and delta formation (A.1), contributing to the hypsography of continental surfaces.
Erosion by water, wind, and glaciers shapes the vertical relief and physical features of the Earth's surface (A.1), including canyon formation and coastal modification.
Earth's southernmost continent, relevant to physiography of continents (A.2), with geology spanning Paleozoic to present and ice sheet dynamics covering a major continental landmass.
Arid landscapes covering roughly one-third of Earth's land surface, relevant to physiography of the continents (A.2), with geomorphic features including plateaus, basins, and sand dunes.
World's largest continent by area, directly relevant to the physiography of the continents (A.2), with diverse climates, major subregions, and geographic features.
A major river forming the world's second-largest delta system, relevant to the physiography of the Asian continent (A.2) and coastal landform features.

Naturally occurring solid minerals constitute the shield areas (B.1) and the rock types comprising folded mountain ranges and island arcs (F.2.e), foundational to understanding the composition of Earth's surface features.
